topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent hum of the late-night lab was usually a comforting drone, but tonight it felt abrasive against my skull. I leaned back in my chair, the worn leather protesting with a soft squeak, and stared at the screen. The data streams scrolled past, familiar patterns yet somehow alien. My fingers hovered over the keyboard, tracing the lines of code I had just finished compiling. Then, a sudden, cold prickle ran down my spine. I glanced over at the adjacent workstation. Another person, hunched over a monitor displaying the exact same schematic, the identical progress bar frozen at the same milestone. My breath hitched, a shallow intake that did little to steady the sudden tremor in my hands. I pushed my chair back, the scraping sound echoing in the quiet room, and started to pace toward the shared display, my gaze locked on the mirrored work.

[story 2]

A buzzing energy thrummed beneath my skin, making the hairs on my arms stand up. I meticulously checked the calibration settings on the prototype, my movements precise, almost obsessive. Every calculation felt sharp, every decision immediate. I was deep in the weeds of the design, immersed in the intricate details of the mechanism, when I noticed it—a file timestamped seconds before mine, bearing the identical project designation. A strange tightness constricted my chest. I straightened up, my posture suddenly rigid, and forced myself to look across the narrow aisle. There, another figure was meticulously annotating the blueprints, their brow furrowed in concentration. A slow, unnerving realization dawned, and I swallowed hard, my throat suddenly dry.

[story 3]

The air in the workshop felt thin, charged with unspoken tension. I tapped my pen against the drafting table, the rhythm steady, but my focus was fracturing. I re-read the preliminary notes, my eyes scanning the complex diagrams with an intensity that bordered on painful. A distinct, unwelcome sensation of hyper-vigilance settled over me; every shadow seemed to shift, every ambient noise amplified. Then, I saw it—a section of the design I had just finalized, replicated perfectly on the adjacent desk. My jaw tightened. I stopped writing, the pen dropping onto the paper with a sharp clatter. I didn't move immediately, just watched the other person across the room, a strange, unsettling certainty blooming in my gut.
